title: The Textures of Time: Agency and Temporal Experience By Michael G. Flaherty Temple University Press. 2010. 192 pages. $27.95 paper
abstract: In his inventive new book, Michael Flaherty continues his work to draw the study of temporality away from the biological and psychological, and into the social. In doing so, he demonstrates that perceptions of time need not be confined to the standpoint of neuroscience or biology, but are moderated by the interplay of self and situation. In The Textures of Time: Agency and Temporal Experience, Flaherty is able to profitably use the framework of temporality to address the broader sociological problem of the existence and evidence of agency. Arguing that challenges in identifying agency have been empirical, not theoretical, Flaherty suggests that the ambiguity surrounding agentic action can be resolved in the “close, systematic observation of agency” in everyday life (6). He does just this by drawing from over 400 semi-structured interviews with people asked to describe moments during which they attempted to customize their experience of time. Through varied examples supplied by the ephemera of daily experience, from standing in the shower to sitting through a dull lecture, he demonstrates how actors attempt to take control of their time. The extensive data aptly demonstrate that day-to-day life is replete with such moments during which individuals act to influence their temporal circumstances, and thus the outcome of certain events. Flaherty reveals that it is precisely in these moments of micro interaction that agency is evident and effective.
